title: The Twenty-Third Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: November 17, 2019

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the Twenty-Third Sunday after Pentecost in Ordinary Time, approaching the final Sundays before Advent (which begins December 1, 2019). The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date would have been Zephaniah 1:7, 12-18; Psalm 90:1-8, 12; 1 Thessalonians 5:1-11; and Matthew 25:14-30 (the Parable of the Talents). These texts carry eschatological themes about preparedness and faithful stewardship as the liturgical year moves toward its conclusion.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of its 79th General Convention (July 2018) which had approved significant canons on marriage equality and affirmed LGBTQ+ inclusion, creating ongoing tension in some conservative dioceses. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert Wright (elected 2017), was navigating these national church shifts while managing a moderate Anglo-Catholic cathedral in downtown Denver. No major diocesan elections or crises were occurring at this specific moment, though the broader polarization within Anglicanism over sexuality and biblical interpretation remained a steady undercurrent.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the midst of its typical late autumn, with temperatures dropping (snow was forecast for the following week). The city was focused on preparations for the Thanksgiving holiday and winter season. The Colorado legislature was in recess, with no active session underway, leaving local news cycles relatively lighter, though ongoing discussions about homelessness and housing affordability in the Capitol Hill neighborhood (where the Cathedral is located) were persistent community concerns.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The impeachment inquiry into President Trump dominated national headlines, with the House Intelligence Committee conducting public hearings on the Ukraine matter (November 13-15 just before this sermon). The 2020 Democratic primary was intensifying with Iowa caucuses only 2.5 months away, generating significant campaign coverage. The economy was showing mixed signals with concerns about manufacturing slowdown, though the unemployment rate remained low at 3.5%.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Hong Kong protests were entering a critical phase with violent clashes between demonstrators and police intensifying throughout November, drawing international attention and condemnation. The British general election was officially called for December 12, 2019, with Boris Johnson's Conservative Party campaigning on the Brexit platform. The U.S.-China trade war continued with ongoing tariff disputes and negotiations unresolved.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The zeitgeist was one of political polarization and uncertainty, with Americans deeply divided over impeachment, the upcoming election, and fundamental questions about democratic norms. The culture was increasingly fractured along partisan lines, though many were attempting to focus on the holiday season ahead. Locally in Denver, the Broncos were in a disappointing 3-7 season, and the Nuggets had just begun their NBA season; cultural attention was beginning its annual shift toward Christmas and year-end reflections.
